2. Report Structure & Format.section_structure: 5/5 — all key sections present (4/4), 35 headings total

3. Content Completeness.technology_evolution: 5/5 — Clearly traces the progression from concatenative synthesis and HMM-based parametric TTS to WaveNet as the deep-learning inflection point, then covers 2020–2022 diversification (FastSpeech 2, VITS, early diffusion) and 2023–present large-scale pretraining/neural codec LM era (VALL-E/VALL-E 2, NaturalSpeech 2) with concrete examples and contextual explanation.
3. Content Completeness.architecture_analysis: 5/5 — Provides substantive technical discussion across multiple paradigm families: autoregressive (VALL-E transformer decoder; Flowtron), non-autoregressive (FastSpeech 2; Glow-TTS), diffusion (Diff-TTS, ProDiff, NaturalSpeech 2, Schrodinger Bridges), and flow/hybrids (Glow-TTS, SimpleSpeech 2, VITS). Includes principles (codec tokens, causal attention, monotonic alignment search, denoising diffusion, distillation/accelerated sampling, latent diffusion) and representative works well beyond simple name listing.
3. Content Completeness.technical_challenges: 5/5 — Covers key challenges explicitly: speed–quality tradeoff, expressive diversity, cross-lingual/cross-speaker generalization, and data efficiency/low-resource. For each, it provides concrete solution directions tied to cited methods (distillation, latent diffusion, parallel decoding, grouped code modeling, style tokens/decoupled codecs, multilingual training, large-scale pretraining/transfer), meeting the required depth and breadth.
3. Content Completeness.evaluation_framework: 4/5 — Includes subjective evaluation (MOS, A/B and preference tests, best-practice notes) and objective metrics (MCD, F0-related measures, speaker-embedding cosine similarity, WER), plus a security/robustness evaluation angle (ASVspoof). However, it omits several commonly cited objective speech-quality/intelligibility metrics explicitly requested (e.g., PESQ, STOI, FAD) and provides limited comparative discussion of metric pros/cons and frontier evaluation methods beyond brief remarks.
3. Content Completeness.future_directions: 4/5 — Presents multiple plausible directions (RLHF, multimodal conditioning, personalization/continual learning with privacy, efficiency/edge deployment, watermarking/detection/access control). It is trend-grounded and somewhat specific (e.g., DLPO). However, it lacks a clear short/medium/long-term roadmap with prioritized milestones, dependencies, and technical feasibility constraints to fully meet the highest bar.

4. Technical Depth.math_formulas: 0/5 — The report provides descriptive explanations of architectures (diffusion, flows, VAE/GAN, transformers) but includes no explicit mathematical formulas (e.g., ELBO, diffusion forward/reverse equations, flow Jacobians), no algorithm/pseudocode, and no computational complexity (Big-O) analysis.
4. Technical Depth.industry_cases: 1/5 — There is one partial real-world case reference (Microsoft MuLanTTS via Blizzard Challenge 2023 MOS). Other mentions (e.g., VALL-E/VALL-E 2, NaturalSpeech 2) are primarily research models rather than clearly analyzed deployed industry systems; no deployment architecture, serving stack, throughput/latency, cost, or production constraints are provided.
4. Technical Depth.ethics_security: 2/5 — Ethics/safety is mentioned (watermarking, detection, access control) and ASVspoof 5 is cited, but there are no concrete detection system comparisons (AUC/EER), no privacy/regulatory treatment (GDPR/CCPA/DPIA), no fairness analysis, no environmental impact quantification, and no specific technical governance/implementation parameters (e.g., DP epsilon, federated learning design).
4. Technical Depth.quantitative_comparison: 2/5 — The report includes isolated quantitative statements (e.g., Diff-TTS 28× real-time; MuLanTTS MOS 4.3–4.5) and lists common metrics, but lacks systematic tabular comparisons across models (MOS/RTF/params), lacks consistent experimental conditions, and does not analyze root technical causes of metric differences beyond high-level narrative.

5. Academic Standards.IEEE_format: 3/3 — standard IEEE numbered citation format detected
5. Academic Standards.terminology_consistency: 3/4 — Technical terminology is largely accurate and used consistently (e.g., autoregressive/non-autoregressive, diffusion, flow-based, MOS, MCD, WER). Most specialized terms are understandable from context, but several are not defined on first mention (e.g., mel-spectrogram, EnCodec, SASV, SECS), and there is an unexplained bilingual insertion ("which拼接") that breaks terminology consistency. No figures/tables are present, so numbering/labeling cannot be assessed.
5. Academic Standards.writing_style: 3/4 — Overall style is academic, objective, and well-structured with clear sectioning and logical flow. However, frequent first-person plural ("we examine," "we present," "we identify") reduces alignment with a strictly third-person/passive academic style, and some phrasing is mildly promotional/subjective (e.g., "revolutionary transformation," "watershed moment," "unprecedented").

6. Data Analysis & Visualization.image_content_eval: 2/5 — Vision LLM unavailable, giving conservative score

eval_report.comment: Good: Report is mostly complete, main content is well-covered, but some dimensions have room for improvement.
